---
output:
  xaringan::moon_reader:
    css: ["default", "extra.css"]
    lib_dir: libs
    seal: false
    nature:
      highlightStyle: github
      highlightLines: true
      countIncrementalSlides: false
      ratio: '16:9'
---

```{r, echo = FALSE, warning = FALSE, message = FALSE}
##xaringan::inf_mr()
## For offline work: https://bookdown.org/yihui/rmarkdown/some-tips.html#working-offline
## Images not appearing? Put images folder inside the libs folder as that is the main data directory

library(tidyverse)
library(readxl)
library(stargazer)
##library(kableExtra)
##library(modelr)

knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE,
                      eval = TRUE,
                      error = FALSE,
                      message = FALSE,
                      warning = FALSE,
                      comment = NA)
```

class: slideblue

.size70[**Today's Agenda**]

<br>

.size50[

Explore the liberal peace debate

- Kant (1798)
- Doyle (1983)

]

<br>

.center[.size40[
  Justin Leinaweaver (Spring 2022)
]]

???

## Prep for Class
1. ...

<br>

*Opening Discussion*

### Anything interesting going on in world politics at the moment?

<br>

### Everybody have the readings for today?



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/08-3-peace_dove_mural.jpg')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

???

Today we explore one of the biggest implications of many liberal models of international relations.

- The existence, or not, of a liberal peace.

<br>

The fundamental idea here is that the spread of democratic institutions and economic exchange leads us to a more peaceful world.

- Interestingly, this is an argument with very, very old roots.

<br>

Let's start with the excerpt from Immanuel Kant.

### What is Kant's goal in this article?
- (Help the world achieve perpetual peace)

### And what does "perpetual" peace mean?
- (Never ending)


---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-triangles_grey_v2.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.size40[.center[**Kant (1798): Perpetual Peace**]]

<br>

### The First Definitive Article for Perpetual Peace
.size30["The Civil Constitution of Every State Should Be Republican"]

### The Second Definitive Article for Perpetual Peace
.size30["The Law of Nations Shall be Founded on a Federation of Free States"]

### The Third Definitive Article for Perpetual Peace
.size30["The Law of World Citizenship Shall Be Limited to Conditions of Universal Hospitality"]

???

Kant argues that adopting these three principles will eventually lead us to a world of perpetual peace.

- I think we'd all agree that everlasting peace is a nice goal.

The question is, how close does Kant get us to achieving that goal?

- The Doyle (1983) article presents counter-arguments to each of these.

Let's dig into both sides on each of these.

<br>

*split class into three groups and assign one article to each*

Go sit with your groups, groups sit in front of the boards.

Groups, you will take some time now to analyze the pros and cons of your assigned article


---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-triangles_grey_v2.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.size60[.center[**Kant (1798) vs Doyle (1983)**]]

<br>

<br>

.center[.size55[Would the global adoption of this article advance the cause of global peace?]]

???



Groups, you have 20 minutes to prepare an answer to this question: 

- Would the global adoption of this article advance the cause of global peace?

- You have to work directly on the board to make two lists: Pros vs Cons

<br>

### Questions?

Get to work!

<br>

*Make sure they work directly on the board so you can push them*

*If one group finishes too quick, send them to help another group*



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-triangles_grey_v2.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.size40[.center[**Kant (1798) vs Doyle (1983): Perpetual Peace**]]

<br>

### The First Definitive Article for Perpetual Peace
.size30["The Civil Constitution of Every State Should Be Republican"]

???

Ok, Group 1, define the article for us.

### Broad strokes, define this type of constitution for me.

(Government in two dimensions)
1. Who holds sovereign power?
- Autocracy (held by one) vs Aristocracy (held by a few) vs Democracy (sovereignty held by all in society)

2. Two possible forms of government: Republican vs Despotic
- Republican: Separates the executive from the legislative power
- Despotic: Government makes and enforces its own laws

<br>

Ok, everybody take some time to review the Kant argument and the Doyle counter-argument on only the first definitive article.

In a few minutes we'll make a pro vs con list on the board.


*ON BOARD*

#### (PRO:)
1. Organized well to meet threats; unity; 
2. Separation of powers tames the ambitions of selfish and aggressive individuals.; 
3. Consent of the people to war is hard to win because they themselves must pay the costs of war themselves.; 
4. Prevents despotism.
5. (Doyle's idea) Regular rotation of office prevents personal animosities between heads of state from escalating across time.

#### (Con:)
1. At best, creates "caution" not "peace."; 
2. "organized well" can lead to constant preparation for war that can enhance military institutions to the point they take over a society
3. Indistinct argument; the logic is very general; 
4. Contradictory evidence; Some security obsessed states adopt liberal constraints over time.

<br>

### What is despotism and why do we want to avoid it?
(``...the exercise of absolute power, especially in a cruel and oppressive way.'')

### How is a direct democracy despotic like a dictatorship?
(Majority holds all of the power (exec and leg), can tyrannize over any minority)

### Do we buy this? is direct democracy likely to become despotic?



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-triangles_grey_v2.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.size40[.center[**Kant (1798) vs Doyle (1983): Perpetual Peace**]]

<br>

### The Second Definitive Article for Perpetual Peace
.size30["The Law of Nations Shall be Founded on a Federation of Free States"]

???

### Broad strokes, define this federation for me.

<br>

*ON BOARD*

#### (PRO:)
1. Cooperation begets cooperation;
2. "League" not a "treaty" ends all wars not just one without imposing new civil laws on the powers of a state, just maintains peace between them
3. NOT a world state, that is tyranny
4. Demonstrated success will lead other states to join, widening the league
5. Encourage diplomacy

#### (Con:)
1. Liberalism is not inherently peace-loving; free states will not necessarily be restrained or act in pursuit of peace
2. Liberal states may, in fact, be more aggressive toward nonliberal states. 



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-triangles_grey_v2.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.size40[.center[**Kant (1798) vs Doyle (1983): Perpetual Peace**]]

<br>

### The Third Definitive Article for Perpetual Peace
.size30["The Law of World Citizenship Shall Be Limited to Conditions of Universal Hospitality"]

???

### Define this for me.

<br>

*ON BOARD*

#### (PRO:)
1. More interactions, more communication should equal greater levels of peacefulness and comity.
2. NOT a right to settle or plunder, more about allowing voluntary exchanges of goods and ideas

#### (Con:)
1. How does universal hospitality not create powerful identity crises?
2. Voiceless minority in each country (no say in government as a long-term visitor) is approaching despotism for that government, right?

<br>

Generally speaking, Doyle seems enthusiastic when reframing the third article to be more about cosmopolitan law.

### What is cosmpolitan law and why is he enthusiastic about its capacity to foster peace?

(Every state embracing a "spirit of commerce" adds material incentives to the need to avoid war.)

- Trade means the greatest benefit comes from working together
- States no longer have to meddle in production and distribution questions, leave it to the businesses!



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-triangles_grey_v2.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.size40[.center[**Kant (1798) vs Doyle (1983): Perpetual Peace**]]

<br>

### The First Definitive Article for Perpetual Peace
.size30["The Civil Constitution of Every State Should Be Republican"]

### The Second Definitive Article for Perpetual Peace
.size30["The Law of Nations Shall be Founded on a Federation of Free States"]

### The Third Definitive Article for Perpetual Peace
.size30["The Law of World Citizenship Shall Be Limited to Conditions of Universal Hospitality"]

???

### All in all, which side do you find more convincing? Why?

<br>

### What are the strongest part's of Kant's argument?

#### - Which would be the easiest one to implement? Why?

<br>

### What are the weakest parts?

#### - Which would be the hardest to implement? Why?




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-triangles_grey_v2.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.size25[
1. No secret treaty of peace shall be held valid in which there is tacitly reserved matter for a future war

2. No independent states, large or small, shall come under the dominion of another state by inheritance, exchange, purchase, or donation

3. Standing armies shall in time be totally abolished

4. National debts shall not be contracted with a view to the external friction of states

5. No state shall by force interfere with the constitution or government of another state

6. No state shall, during war, permit such acts of hostility which would make mutual confidence in the subsequent peace impossible: such are the employment of assassins (percussores), poisoners (venefici), breach of capitulation, and incitement to treason (perduellio) in the opposing state
]

???

So, here's the deal, in Kant's full essay he proposes six preliminary articles that must be adopted in order for his main three articles to achieve peace.

Notes on 1) No reservations or agreements to decide contentious issues later. Settle everything now.

Notes on 2) A state is not property, it is a society of men who no one outside their state has a right to command or dispose of.

Notes on 3) Also applies to bulding up of massive treasuries, building up these resources is a threat to others.

Notes on 4) Trade built on credit systems (blames England explicitly) leads to amassing treasure and seeking war

Notes on 5) However, you can jump in and pick sides in a civil war. For some reason that's not a problem.

Notes on 6) ?


---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-triangles_grey_v2.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.size25[
1. No secret treaty of peace shall be held valid in which there is tacitly reserved matter for a future war

2. No independent states, large or small, shall come under the dominion of another state by inheritance, exchange, purchase, or donation

3. Standing armies shall in time be totally abolished

4. National debts shall not be contracted with a view to the external friction of states

5. No state shall by force interfere with the constitution or government of another state

6. No state shall, during war, permit such acts of hostility which would make mutual confidence in the subsequent peace impossible: such are the employment of assassins (percussores), poisoners (venefici), breach of capitulation, and incitement to treason (perduellio) in the opposing state
]

???

### Do you think adding these preliminary articles make the plan stronger or weaker? Why?

#### - Which would be the easiest ones to implement? Why?

#### - Which would be the hardest to implement? Why?

<br>

### Overall, has Kant given us a plan that, if implemented, is likely to succeed? Why or why not?

<br>

### Has Kant given us a plan that is impossible to implement? Why or why not?

<br>

### In what ways is this similar to Institutionalism?

<br>

### In what ways is this similar to Economic Liberalism?



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_triangles2.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.size70[**Next Week: Paper 2**]

<br>

.size55[.center[Should the US use force (military or economic) to compel other states to behave in line with our interests?]]

???

Next week we work on your next paper assignment for the class.

### Questions on the prompt?

<br>

SLIDE: To get us started...




---

class: middle, slideblue

.size60[**For Monday**]

<br>

.size35[.center[**Should the US use force (military or economic) to compel other states to behave in line with our interests?**]]

.size35[
Bring one piece of .textred[**HIGH QUALITY**] published evidence you can use to answer the question.

Be ready to provide the class with a correctly formatted, .textred[**APA style citation**] for your piece of evidence (see the Purdue OWL for guidance).
]

???

Perhaps an example of a country somewhere using force to successfully achieve good outcomes in the world?

Or an example of the opposite? An action that bit a country in the butt?

### Questions on the prompt for Monday?
